# Training session backup checklist
**Session name:** ____________  
**Date:** ____________  
**Trainer:** ____________  
**Backup person (if any):** ____________

## 1) Prep the day before
- [ ] Final agenda printed **by section / time**
- [ ] Editable master saved locally
- [ ] PDF backup saved locally
- [ ] Files saved in a second location
- [ ] Printed outline / speaker notes
- [ ] Attendance sheet
- [ ] Key handout / reference sheet
- [ ] Required policy language printed, if needed
- [ ] Short talk track for main points
- [ ] Marker / pen
- [ ] Phone charger / power bank
- [ ] Backup person knows their role

## 2) Grab right before the session
- [ ] Laptop + charger
- [ ] Printed agenda
- [ ] Printed outline / notes
- [ ] Attendance sheet
- [ ] Handouts
- [ ] Policy language page, if needed
- [ ] Marker / pen
- [ ] Backup file access saved locally

## 3) First 5 minutes if tech fails
### Decision order
1. **Try the quick fix**
   - restart device
   - reconnect cable
   - switch display source
2. **Switch source**
   - open local file
   - use PDF backup
3. **Go paper**
   - use printed agenda and outline
   - talk through the main points
4. **Reschedule only if needed**
   - only if the session cannot be delivered safely or at all

### If the laptop dies completely
- [ ] Stop troubleshooting after a quick check
- [ ] Use printed materials
- [ ] Use whiteboard / flip chart if available
- [ ] Deliver the session from the outline
- [ ] Collect attendance manually

### If file access fails but projector still works
- [ ] Use local files first
- [ ] If local files fail, use PDF backup
- [ ] If the file still won’t open, switch to paper and whiteboard
- [ ] Keep the projector only if it helps

## 4) What to keep on paper no matter what
- [ ] Attendance
- [ ] Agenda by section / time
- [ ] Key points
- [ ] Required policy language
- [ ] Speaker notes / talk track
- [ ] Contact list for follow-up

## 5) What “paper-based delivery” means
- Read or summarize the agenda section by section
- Use the printed key points as your guide
- Write important items on the board
- Ask simple check-in questions
- Keep the session moving without slides

## 6) What to drop first
- [ ] Slide-heavy detail
- [ ] Extra examples
- [ ] Optional discussion
- [ ] Anything that needs live file access

## 7) Minimum session to deliver
- Welcome
- Purpose of the session
- Main points people need now
- Required policy items
- Where to get help
- Next steps

Agent: Absolutely — here’s a **plug-and-play time-block template** that assumes **Riverside County traffic, parking, and small delays**, so it’s built with buffers instead of just mentioning them.

## Simple daily template

### Morning
- **[Time]** Wake up / get ready
- **[Time] – [Time]** Commute to first stop  
  - **Buffer built in:** leave **15–30 minutes earlier** than the “ideal” drive time
- **[Time] – [Time]** Call window #1  
  - Best if you’re parked or stationary
- **[Time] – [Time]** Errand #1  
  - Put the most time-sensitive errand first

### Midday
- **[Time] – [Time]** Travel to next area  
  - Assume traffic + parking delay
- **[Time] – [Time]** Errand #2
- **[Time] – [Time]** Buffer block  
  - Use this if the first errand runs long
- **[Time] – [Time]** Call window #2

### Afternoon / evening
- **[Time] – [Time]** Commute to final stop or home
- **[Time] – [Time]** Errand #3 or pickup
- **[Time] – [Time]** Final buffer / cleanup block
- **[Time] – [Time]** Wrap-up / prep for next day

---

## Example with built-in delay assumptions

If you want a real-looking version, use this structure:

- **7:30–8:15** Get ready / leave early
- **8:15–9:00** Commute  
  - Planned for a 30-minute drive, but padded for Riverside traffic
- **9:00–9:30** Call #1
- **9:30–10:15** Errand #1
- **10:15–10:30** Buffer
- **10:30–11:15** Drive to next stop
- **11:15–11:45** Errand #2
- **11:45–12:15** Lunch / call window #2
- **12:15–1:00** Commute / parking / transition
- **1:00–1:30** Errand #3
- **1:30–2:00** Buffer for delays
- **2:00–2:45** Return commute
- **2:45–3:00** Flex block

---

## Easy rule for Riverside-style traffic
Use this formula for each stop:

**Drive time + 15 minutes + parking time + 10 minutes buffer**

So if a drive normally takes 25 minutes, block **50 minutes** if you need to be safe.

---

## Best order for the day
If you’re trying to avoid running late, this order usually works best:

1. **Hard-time call**
2. **Most time-sensitive errand**
3. **Second errand on the same route**
4. **Flexible errand**
5. **Final buffer before the next commitment**

---

## Copy/paste template
You can fill this in quickly:

- **[Time]–[Time]** Commute to first stop
- **[Time]–[Time]** Call #1
- **[Time]–[Time]** Errand #1
- **[Time]–[Time]** Buffer / drive
- **[Time]–[Time]** Errand #2
- **[Time]–[Time]** Call #2
- **[Time]–[Time]** Drive / pickup / drop-off
- **[Time]–[Time]** Final buffer
- **[Time]–[Time]** Home / wrap-up

If you want, I can turn this into a **specific schedule for your actual errands and calls** if you send me:
- start time
- commute length
- errand list
- call times
- where you’re starting from and going to

Agent: Yes — here’s a simple **two-phase plan** you can use when a few things go wrong at once.

# 1) Immediate issue: stabilize first
Focus only on what stops the day from getting worse.

## First 5 minutes
- [ ] Identify the most urgent problem
- [ ] Stop anything that depends on the broken piece
- [ ] Check what’s actually affected
- [ ] Decide what can keep going without delay

## Next 10–15 minutes
- [ ] Switch to the simplest workable option
- [ ] Move the task to a backup location or format if needed
- [ ] Confirm who needs to know right now
- [ ] Keep a short note of what changed

## Hard rule
Don’t try to fix everything at once.  
Handle the issue that blocks the most important task first.

---

# 2) Follow-up: clean up after the immediate problem
Once things are stable, deal with the rest in order.

## Follow-up sequence
1. [ ] Tell the relevant person what happened
2. [ ] Note what was delayed or skipped
3. [ ] Send or recreate any missing materials
4. [ ] Update anyone who was waiting on the result
5. [ ] Reset the plan for the rest of the day

---

# Simple priority order
Use this order if multiple things are off:
1. **Safety / hard deadline**
2. **Most time-sensitive task**
3. **Task that affects other people**
4. **Everything else**

---

# Quick template
**Immediate fix:**  
- Problem:  
- Action taken:  
- Backup used:  
- Who was notified:  

**Follow-up:**  
- What still needs attention:  
- Who gets updated:  
- By when:  

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist** or make it specific to your actual situation.

Agent: Absolutely — here’s a simple **car-issue / insurance checklist** you can keep handy.

## 1) Right away
- [ ] Pull over safely if needed
- [ ] Turn on hazards
- [ ] Check for injuries
- [ ] If anyone is hurt, call emergency services

## 2) If it’s a minor incident
- [ ] Move to a safe spot if the car still works
- [ ] Take photos of the car, damage, plates, and scene
- [ ] Save the time and location
- [ ] Get the other driver’s info if another car is involved
- [ ] Note any witnesses

## 3) What to save
- [ ] Photos
- [ ] Dashcam footage, if you have it
- [ ] Insurance info
- [ ] Driver’s license info
- [ ] License plate number
- [ ] Police or incident report number, if there is one
- [ ] Tow or roadside receipt, if needed

## 4) Follow-up
- [ ] Report it to your insurance company
- [ ] Keep copies of everything you send
- [ ] Write down who you talked to and when
- [ ] Save repair estimates and receipts

## 5) Simple rule
If the car is **unsafe, smoking, leaking, or making a bad noise**, don’t keep driving it.

- [ ] Extra examples
- [ ] Optional discussion

Can you help me lay out the basic order of things to do right after an accident, plus what usually needs to get documented for insurance?

Agent: Yes — here’s a simple **two-phase plan** you can use when a few things go wrong at once.

# 1) Immediate issue: stabilize first
Focus only on what stops the day from getting worse.

## First 5 minutes
- [ ] Identify the most urgent problem
- [ ] Stop anything that depends on the broken piece
- [ ] Check what’s actually affected
- [ ] Decide what can keep going without delay

## Next 10–15 minutes
- [ ] Switch to the simplest workable option
- [ ] Move the task to a backup location or format if needed
- [ ] Confirm who needs to know right now
- [ ] Keep a short note of what changed

## Hard rule
Don’t try to fix everything at once.  
Handle the issue that blocks the most important task first.

---

# 2) Follow-up: clean up after the immediate problem
Once things are stable, deal with the rest in order.

## Follow-up sequence
1. [ ] Tell the relevant person what happened
2. [ ] Note what was delayed or skipped
3. [ ] Send or recreate any missing materials
4. [ ] Update anyone who was waiting on the result
5. [ ] Reset the plan for the rest of the day

---

# Simple priority order
Use this order if multiple things are off:
1. **Safety / hard deadline**
2. **Most time-sensitive task**
3. **Task that affects other people**
4. **Everything else**

---

# Quick template
**Immediate fix:**  
- Problem:  
- Action taken:  
- Backup used:  
- Who was notified:  

**Follow-up:**  
- What still needs attention:  
- Who gets updated:  
- By when:

Agent: Yes — the safest way is to build the session around **what must happen**, then add a few **fallbacks** for the most likely failures.

## 1) Define the core goal
Write one sentence for the session:
- “By the end, attendees should know who we are, what they need this week, and how to get help.”

That keeps the session from depending on perfect room setup or materials.

## 2) Use a 3-layer backup plan

### Layer 1: Room setup fails
If the projector, screen, or seating arrangement is off:
- Switch to **discussion-led**
- Use a **whiteboard or flip chart**
- Hand out printed notes if available
- Keep the agenda on one sheet you can hold or read from

### Layer 2: Materials fail
If slides, packets, or handouts aren’t ready:
- Use a **talking outline**
- Cover only the essentials:
  1. Welcome
  2. What we do
  3. What matters this week
  4. How to work here
  5. Questions
- Ask one or two simple check-in questions instead of relying on visuals

### Layer 3: Attendee list is wrong
If the people in the room don’t match the plan:
- Start with a quick roll call or intro round
- Ask who is brand new vs. returning
- Adjust examples on the spot
- Skip anything too role-specific unless it applies to everyone

## 3) Keep a “minimum viable session”
Have a version that works in 20–30 minutes if everything is messy:
1. Welcome and purpose
2. Key info for this week
3. Basic expectations
4. Where to ask questions
5. Close and next steps

## 4) Prepare a tiny backup kit
Bring or have ready:
- Printed agenda
- One-page session outline
- Marker
- Blank paper or notepad
- Attendance sheet
- Contact list for follow-up
- Any critical handout in paper form

## 5) Decide what gets cut first
If time or setup is bad, cut in this order:
1. Nice-to-have examples
2. Deep detail
3. Optional discussion
4. Anything that depends on slides

Keep:
- Safety / policy essentials
- This week’s priorities
- Who to contact for help

## 6) Recovery move if multiple things fail at once
Use this sequence:
1. Acknowledge the issue briefly
2. Switch to the simplest format available
3. Cover only the essential points
4. Confirm understanding
5. Send follow-up materials after the session

## Simple fallback script
“Looks like we’re missing a few setup pieces, so I’m going to keep this simple and cover the essentials directly. By the end, you’ll know what matters this week and where to go for help.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page contingency plan** you can print and bring with you.
